text: This introductory chapter lays out the foundational thesis of Bruno's directional picking system: when crossing to a higher string use a down-stroke, when crossing to a lower string use an up-stroke, and when staying on one string use alternate strokes (Core picking rules). Bruno justifies the rule through economy of motion (Motivation for the rule) and explicitly frames the method as breaking the entrenched alternate-picking habit (Breaking the alternate-picking habit), warning that practice will feel awkward before paying off. The chapter establishes the load-bearing mechanical preconditions (Right-hand position): a relaxed, still wrist with motion driven from the elbow and fingers not touching the strings or guitar body. It then catalogs the stroke vocabulary used throughout the book - down-up, up-down, and the difficult in-between stroke (Stroke types catalog) - and introduces consecutive down/up strokes with their characteristic rushing failure mode (Consecutive strokes). Finally it lays down the usage rules that govern every subsequent exercise: a symbols legend for picking notation, practicing each exercise starting with both strokes for symmetry, a tempo prescription of MM 60-120, and a sequential study order with no skipping around.
